stem: A colony of bacteria triples in population every 4 hours. The population at time $t = 0$ hours is $400$ bacteria. The population is modeled by $P(t) = 400 \cdot 3^{t/4}$. After how many complete hours will the population first reach at least $32{,}400$ bacteria?
explanation: Set $P(t) \geq 32{,}400$: $400 \cdot 3^{t/4} \geq 32{,}400$. Dividing both sides by 400: $3^{t/4} \geq 81$. Since $81 = 3^4$, we need $t/4 \geq 4$, so $t \geq 16$. Verification: $P(16) = 400 \cdot 3^{16/4} = 400 \cdot 3^4 = 400 \cdot 81 = 32{,}400 \geq 32{,}400$. $\checkmark$ The population first reaches at least $32{,}400$ after $16$ complete hours.

distractorRationale.A: Error: setting $3^{t/4} = 3^4$ and concluding $t = 4$ by reading off the exponent directly without accounting for the factor of $\frac{1}{4}$ in the exponent. A student who writes $\frac{t}{4} = 4$ but then reports $t = 4$ (the right-hand exponent) instead of solving $t = 4 \times 4 = 16$ would get $4$.
distractorRationale.B: Error: using a tripling period of 3 hours instead of 4 hours. A student who misreads the model and writes $3^{t/3} = 81 = 3^4$ obtains $t/3 = 4$, giving $t = 12$.
distractorRationale.D: Error: solving $3^{t/4} = 3^4 \Rightarrow t/4 = 4$ correctly, but then computing $t = 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 20$ (adding 4 five times) instead of $t = 4 \times 4 = 16$. This arithmetic error of adding 4 one extra time gives $t = 20$.
